coredata.dc:title: Electrophoretic extraction of highly monodispersed graphene quantum dots from widely polydispersed bulk and its cytotoxicity effect against cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Davardoostmanesh, Maryam
Ahmadzadeh, Hossein
Goharshadi, Elaheh K.
Meshkini, Azadeh
Sistanipour, Elnaz
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we used a rapid (less than 10 s), inexpensive, and efficient method for exteraction of graphene quantum dots, GQDs, after thermal pyrolysis preparation from citric acid. The electrophoretic size fractionation was performed following the citric acid pyrolysis without using dialysis membrane. The last separated fraction was GQDs with a narrow size distribution and average size of ~60 nm. The characterization techniques showed that the GQDs exhibit strong photoluminescence properties which are highly desired for several applications in various fields such as cancer therapy. The cytotoxicity assesments demonstrated that the GQDs have higher toxicity (IC50 of 31.08 ± 2.71 µg/mL after 24 h) against human bone carcinoma cell line, Saos-2, compared to the bulk (IC50 of 65.06 ± 5.41 µg/mL after 24 h).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Electrophoretic size fractionation
Photoluminescence
Cancer therapy
Saos-2 cell lines

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge Ferdowsi University of Mashhad for supporting this project (grant number 3/46638).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the bulk and GQDs. (b) The band gap energies for the bulk and GQDs. (c) PL spectra for the bulk and GQDs in the visible range under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. (d) The fluorescence microscopy images of the bulk and GQDs with magnification of 200x.
(a) The XRD pattern of GQDs. (b) The zeta potentials of the bulk and GQDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of the bulk and GQDs. (d) The elemental mapping of GQDs.

The TEM images of (a) the bulk, (b) GQDs, The FE-SEM images of (c) the bulk, (d) GQDs, the AFM topography images with the corresponding SSD for (e) the bulk and (f) GQDs. The histogram bin size for all fractions was chosen as 50. The height profile to each AFM image is shown in the adjacent section on the right side.

(a) The cell viability percentage of Saos-2 cells obtained from MTT assay after incubation with various concentrations of the bulk and GQDs for 24 h. (b) The fluorescence microscopy images of the Saos-2 cells (control) incubated with the samples (bulk and GQDs) for 4 h (magnification: 200x). The cells were stained with Hoechst 33,342 for nucleus. (c) SEM images of Saos-2 cells incubated with the bulk and GQDs for 24 h.

head.ce:title: Electrophoretic extraction of highly monodispersed graphene quantum dots from widely polydispersed bulk and its cytotoxicity effect against cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad 9177948974, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad 9177948974, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Nano Research, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad 9177948974, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Nano Research, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad 9177948974, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thermal pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) was performed at high temperature. The electrophoretic size fractionation of bulk carbonaceous nanomaterials was carreid out in continuation of the CA pyrolysis. The smalest collected fraction was GQDs. The cytotoxicity assesments demonstrated that the GQDs have higher toxicity against bone cancer cells than bulk. SEM microscopy images showed that the morphological changes in GQDs-treated cells are greater than bulk-treated cells.

In this work, we used a rapid (less than 10 s), inexpensive, and efficient method for exteraction of graphene quantum dots, GQDs, after thermal pyrolysis preparation from citric acid. The electrophoretic size fractionation was performed following the citric acid pyrolysis without using dialysis membrane. The last separated fraction was GQDs with a narrow size distribution and average size of ~60 nm. The characterization techniques showed that the GQDs exhibit strong photoluminescence properties which are highly desired for several applications in various fields such as cancer therapy. The cytotoxicity assesments demonstrated that the GQDs have higher toxicity (IC50 of 31.08 ± 2.71 µg/mL after 24 h) against human bone carcinoma cell line, Saos-2, compared to the bulk (IC50 of 65.06 ± 5.41 µg/mL after 24 h).

ce:section.ce:para: Carbonaceous nanomaterials (CNs) consist of a broad class of nanostructures composed of sp2-bonded graphitic carbons. CNs may be divided into four major categories including zero-dimensional (D) carbon quantum dots (CQDs), one-D carbon nanotubes (CNTs), two-D graphene, and three–D aerogel-based CNs [1]. CNs have several attractive properties such as good optical transparency, high electrical conductivity, and excellent thermal conductivity [2–4]. These unique properties make CNs useful for several applications over a wide range of areas including optoelectronics [5], electrochemistry [6], solar cells [7], fuel cells [8], and medicine [9–13]. The properties of CNs are strongly dependent on their size. For example, large sizes of two-D CNs are highly desirable for water splitting [14], fabricating three–dimensional networks [15], and preparation of conductive thin films for optoelectronic devices [16]. Due to the large sizes of two-D CNs, their applications in the field of biomedicine are not well developed.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are classified as zero-D CNs with sizes less than 100 nm at least in one dimension and have attracted a broad attention because of their outstanding advantages such as stable photoluminescence (PL) properties, biocompatibility, versatile surface chemistry, and low toxicity [17,18]. There is a difference between the GQDs and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). CQDs have sizes less than 10 nm [19]. The chemical and physical methods were used for preparation of zero-D CNs with a tunable size. Chemical methods include electrochemical [20], hydrothermal/solvothermal [21], thermal decomposition [22], and microwave assisted cleaving [23]. Physical methods include arc discharge [24], laser ablation/passivation, and plasma [25,26]. However, most of these methods suffer from some drawbacks including the use of harsh conditions, time consuming procedures, low yield, and use of expensive equipment. The thermal pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) is one of the most common chemical methods for the synthesis of GQDs [22]. This method is simple and efficient for large scale production of GQDs. However, it requires expensive dialysis membranes and it takes more than 3 days to prepare GQDs.

Historically, separation science is performed either in analytical, semi-preparative, and preparative scales. The preparative scale separation is mainly used in industry involving cost and energy considerations. Several classical methods such as dialysis and centrifugation could separate materials in semi-preparative scale. Although centrifugation is commonly used in laboratories, the resolution is limited to the rpm of the centrifuge so that a high resolution separation requires high rpm and high energy consumption. Size of separated particles in dialysis is limited to a cut-off size of used membranes.

Analytical scale separation is mainly concerned with lowering the amount of sample injected up to single molecule detection and obtaining the highest possible resolution. Instrumental techniques such as electrophoresis and chromatography separate materials in analytical scale. In general, energy consumption is not a major concern at the laboratory scale separation. In instrumental separation methods for example chromatography techniques like size exclusion chromatography, the cost of instrumentation, columns, and solvents is usually high. Among all separation techniques mentioned, electrophoresis offers the fastest separation technique with lowest operational costs and energy consumption. Electrophoresis is usually a non-destructive technique, i.e. the sample components are separated intact. If there are cleavage products in the production process, the products are separated depending on their size and charge. It has been shown that nanoparticles (NPs) and macromolecules such as proteins, mitochondria, and even whole cells are separated intact with no cleavage [27–29]. In spite of several advantages of electrophoresis techniques such as capillary electrophoresis (CE), they suffer from the lack of scalability. Thus, at the expense of significant loss in resolution, the maximum number of separated fractions is achieved.

Herein, we used preparative scale electrophoretic separation and implement it in order to fractionate the highly monodispersed GQDs from a widely polydispersed bulk preparation of CNs to increase the loading capacity with three order of magnitude compared with CE.

In this work, we used the mega-bore electrophoretic size fractionation after the pyrolysis of CA to obtain GQDs from the last collected fraction in a very short time with a high-throughput approach. Although the method used in this work for size fractionation is considered a separation technique in non-linear chromatography/electrophoresis, the results show an efficient preparation technique and capable to extract a highly monodispersed fraction with anticancer properties which is buried in the bulk. The anticancer property of the GQDs was also investigated against bone cancer cell line (Saos-2) and compared with the bulk. This work establishes high-speed, high-throughput, and high-resolution fractionation method for large scale GQDs preparation with potential application in cancer treatment.

Citric acid (CA) (99.5%) from Fluca, sodium hydrogen phosphate (98%), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (99%), and NaOH (greater than 97%) were all purchased from Merck (New Jersey, USA) and used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized (DI) water.

The cell culture medium (RPMI-1640) and penicillin–streptomycin from Biosera, fetal bovine serum (FBS) from Gibco BRL, cancer cell line (Saos-2) from Pasteur Institute, human foreskin fibroblast (HFF) cells from the Research Institute of Biotechnology in Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) from Sigma Chemical Co., dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) from Merck Millipore, Hoechst 33,342 from Tocris Cookson were purchased.

The Electrothermal MY6404 Heating Mantle (200 W) was used for pyrolysis of CA. The ALPHA 1–2 LD PLUS freeze-drying instrument was used for drying the samples. The zeta potential, ζ, of samples was measured using ZetaSizer (Nano-ZS). The electrophoresis device was purchased from Hercules Bio-Rad Laboratories. The UV–vis absorption spectra of samples were recorded using Photonix Ar 2015 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The Olympus-DP71, BX51 fluorescence microscope was used to obtain the fluorescence images of the samples. The photoluminescence spectra were recorded by PerkinElmer LS 45 fluorescence spectrometer. The XRD pattern of GQDs was determined using Bruker D8 Advanced diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. The FT-IR spectra were recorded using Thermo Nicolet Avatar 370 FT-IR Spectrometer scanning from 4000 to 500 cm−1. The chemical composition of the samples was obtained using a Thermo Finnigan (Flash 1112 Series EA) CHN analyzer. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the samples were recorded using a LEO 912 AB instrument. The electron beam accelerating voltage was 120 kV. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of the samples were obtained using 0101/A AraResearch in a non-contact mode at room temperature. The field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images of the samples were obtained using TESCAN-XMU. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the samples were recorded using Leo 1450VP. The absorption of the samples in each well of the ELISA plates was read using Biotech, ELX800 ELISA reader.

The CA pyrolysis was performed in a heating mantle device using a method reported previously by our group [22]. Briefly, 2 g CA was heated to 200 °C. After 5 min, CA was liquated and the color of the liquid changed from colorless to orange. The solution was cooled to room temperature and NaOH (0.1 M) was added slowly to neutralize the solution. After neutralization, the aqueous solution of bulk CNs was obtained. Although it was shown that the CA is carbonized under similar experimental conditions [30]. A complete carbonization occurs under prolonged heating.

The electrophoresis experiments were performed using glass capillary tubes with an inner diameter of 1.1–1.2 mm, outer diameter of 1.5–1.6 mm, and length of 75 mm. The glass capillaries arrayed in a bundle of ten for simultaneous and high-throughput fractionation. Each glass capillary tube was filled with buffer and placed in the electrophoresis chamber. The electrophoretic fractionation was carried out using a 10 mM phosphate buffer at pH 7.4. Then, ~5 μ L of bulk CNs was added into each tube. The synthesized bulk CNs include a large number of carboxylic groups which are deprotonated at pH 7.4. Hence, the bulk CNs has negative charge and migrate to anode. They are separated into several fractions with different sizes. The fractionation was performed in less than 10 s under 40 V separation voltage. Then, the glass tubes were cut using a Sapphire cutter and a syringe plunger was used to collect the last fraction as GQDs into a glass vial.

The widths of the size distribution is determined by the lengths of the glass tubes that could be cut for fraction collection. Although the electrophoresis technique could separate GQDs into individual single species, practically it is not possible to collect individual species of GQDs. In this work, we have manually cut the glass capillary tubes into pieces and have chosen the last fraction with highest mobility (smallest sheets with narrowest size distribution). Using high throughput separation and high loading capacity, the fractions were collected in a high enough scale for further characterization. The GQDs with a yield of about 10% of the bulk were obtained. It has a merit to mention that the electrophoresis is a very mild technique and does not split or change the size of prepared nanomaterials. Excess reagents and incomplete cleavage products of CA may migrate to the appropriate location depending on their size and charge during the electrophoretic separation process. The collected fractions are usually pure and only contain the buffer components. For biological applications, the electrophoretic fractionation was carried out using phosphate buffer at physiological pH 7.4. The buffer components in the collected fraction do not interfere with biological applications.

The human osteosarcoma cell line (Saos-2) was used to test the toxicity of each fraction. Saos-2 cells were cultured in RPMI-1640 medium supplemented with FBS (10%, v/v), streptomycin (100 μg/mL), and penicillin (100 U/mL). The cells were incubated under 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere at 37 °C.

The cytotoxicity of samples was evaluated by MTT colorimetric assay. Saos-2 cells were cultured in 96-well plates with a density of 104 cells/well. After that, the cells were treated with the GQDs and bulk CNs with various concentrations (5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200 µg/mL) for 24 h. Subsequently, 15 μL MTT dye (5 mg/mL) was added to each well and incubated for another 4 h at 37 °C. The purple insoluble formazan crystals were produced as a result of MTT reaction with the viable cells. Finally, MTT solution was removed and formazan sediment was dissolved in 200 μL DMSO. The color intensity of the dye is proportional to the cell viability. The number of viable cells was measured using a spectrophotometer at 570 nm.

The Saos-2 cancer cells were cultivated in a culture plates to grow against the wall of the flasks. Then, 31 μg/mL of the bulk and GQDs fraction were added to the cells. After 4 h, the control and treated cells were centrifuged (6000 rpm, 10 min) and washed with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) several times. Ultimately, the dyed cells were rinsed with PBS and collected for fluorescence microscopy image recording.

The cancer cells seeded on sterile glass cover slips were treated with IC50 concentrations of the bulk and GQDs for 24 h. Then, the cells were fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde and 0.1 M sodium cacodylate buffer solution for 2 h. Subsequently, the cover slips were washed three times with 0.1 M sodium cacodylate buffer (each time 15 min) and dehydrated with 30, 50, 70, 90, and 100% ethanol (each one 15 min). Finally, the cover slips were dried and placed on SEM stubs coated with gold and observed for morphological changes using SEM.

The optical properties of the product of CA pyrolysis (bulk) and fractionated GQDs were examined using UV–vis spectroscopy and the results are shown in Fig. 1 a, b. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the bulk demonstrates a peak at 197 nm attributed to the π → π* electronic transition of aromatic C = C bonds. An outstanding blue shift was observed from 270 nm for the bulk to 250 nm for GQDs because of quantum confinements. The band gaps, Eg , of the bulk and GQDs were calculated using Tauc equation [31]:
(1)
( α h ν )
n
= B
h ν -
E g
where
α
,
h ν
, and
B
are absorption coefficient, photon energy, and a constant related to the material, respectively. The value of n is either 2 or 1/2 for direct or indirect semiconductors, respectively. The GQDs are indirect semiconductors [22]. The band gaps of the bulk and GQDs were calculated as 3.6 and 4.0 eV, respectively. The band gap of the GQDs is greater than that of the bulk due to the quantum confinements. As the size of a semiconductor nanomaterials decreases, the band gap increases. Brus equation [32] gives the relation between the size and band gap of a semiconductor nanomaterial:
(2)
E
g . n
=
E
g . b
+
π
2
ℏ
2
2
R
2
1
m
e
∗
+
1
m
h
∗
-
1.8
e
2
ε R
where Eg,b
E
g a p , b u l k
is the bulk band gap, R is the particle radius,
m
e
∗
and
m
h
∗
are the effective masses of the electron and hole, respectively. h is Plank ́s constant, and ε is the bulk optical dielectric constant. Goharshadi et al. [32] showed the band gap of bulk ZnS (3.54 eV) increases to 6.23 eV for ZnS quantum dots.

Fig. 1c shows the PL emission spectra of the bulk and GQDs at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. A maximum PL emission for the bulk is centered at 470 nm with a bright blue photoluminescence emission. The PL emission peak of GQDs shows a significant blue shift with respect to the bulk because of their much smaller sizes. Fig. 1d shows the fluorescence images of the bulk and GQDs. The blue fluorescence observed in GQDs is brighter than that of the bulk.

The XRD pattern of GQDs shown in Fig. 2 a consists of a broad peak centered at 2θ = 32.4° related to the (002) crystallography plane. The broadness of the peak corresponds to the small sizes of GQDs that confirms the formation of GQDs.

To compare the surface charge of the bulk and GQDs, the zeta potential was measured at different pHs (Fig. 2b). The zeta potential of GQDs is higher than that of the bulk. It indicates the presence of more charged species on the GQDs surface. The functional groups of the bulk and GQDs were determined by the FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2c). The broad band around 3408 cm−1 is attributed to the asymmetrical stretching vibration of O–H group. This band becomes sharper and shows a blue shift in GQDs. The band at 1578 cm−1 originates from the asymmetrical stretching vibration of COO− which confirms the presence of the COOH group. The band at 1390 cm−1 is assigned to C = C bond. The band at 1079 cm−1 is associated with the asymmetrical C-O stretching. This band is more intense for GQDs due to the presence of the more oxygen-containing functional groups in GQDs [33].

The elemental mapping of GQDs shown in Fig. 2d confirms the presence of carbon and oxygen elements. Also, the CHN analysis shows that GQDs were composed of approximately 25.7 wt% carbon and 2.7 wt% hydrogen. The oxygen content was 71.6 wt% which was estimated from the difference between 100% and the sum of carbon + hydrogen.

Fig. 3 a and b represent the TEM images of the bulk and GQDs. The figures clearly demonstrate that the size and thickness of the smallest collected fraction, GQDs, are much less than those of the bulk. The morphology of the bulk and GQDs was determined by FE-SEM and is shown in Fig. 3c and d, respectively. As these figures show the bulk contains stacked and aggregated sheets while GQDs are monodispersed small sheets.

The AFM images and sheet size distribution (SSD) of the bulk and GQDs are presented in Fig. 3e and f, respectively. The SSD was calculated using SPIP software [34]. The AFM images show that the bulk is a mixture of particles with a wide SSD and topographic height of 2.2 nm. The prepared GQDs contain the small sheets with the average size of ~60 nm and narrow SSD with topographic height of 0.7 nm. As Fig. 3a-3f show the TEM, FE-SEM, and AFM images well cross validate each other.

The cytotoxicity of the bulk and GQDs against Saos-2 cells was evaluated by MTT assay. The cultured cells were treated with different concentrations of the bulk and GQDs within 24 h. The cell viability decreased with increasing concentration of the bulk and GQDs (Fig. 4 a). When the concentration of the bulk and GQDs reached to 50 µg/mL, the cell viability of Saos-2 cells reduced to 63.8% and 45.4%, respectively. However, GQDs showed higher toxicity than that of the bulk against Saos-2 cells. The IC50 for the bulk and GQDs after 24 h treatment was calculated as 65.06 ± 5.41 and 31.08 ± 2.71 µg/mL, respectively (p < 0.02). Hence, the IC50 for GQDs is half of the bulk because of its smaller size, and therefore its higher activity.

The cellular uptake of the bulk and GQDs was examined using fluorescence microscopy after 4 h treatment (Fig. 4b). In comparison with the control cells, the bright blue dots correspond to GQDs are widely distributed in the cytoplasm and near the nucleus membrane. This observation suggests the easy penetration of GQDs into the cells. However, the bulk-treated cells showed no penetration. Accumulation of GQDs near the cell nucleus membrane, as it is concluded from the fluorescence images, suggests the possibility of toxicity against cancer cells. The amount of the cellular uptake increases with decreasing of size.

The mechanism on cellular uptake of GQDs is size dependent and it is most likely endocytosis [35]. The endocytosis is divided into two major pathways including phagocytosis and pinocytosis. Phagocytosis is the uptake of large particles such as micro-organism and viruses. On the other hand, pinocytosis is the uptake of small particles which may or may not be energy-dependent [36]. Kumawat et al. [36] showed that the GQDs enter the cells via energy-dependent endocytosis pathway. The size-dependent interaction of NPs with the cell membrane via endocytosis is possibly associated with the membrane-wrapping process [35]. Several studies indicated that optimal NPs radius for cellular uptake is ~50 nm [37–40]. NPs uptake was shown to decrease for smaller or larger particles. A comparison of toxicity effects of GQDs with graphitic carbon nitrid nanosheets (g-C3N4) in our previous work [41] against Saos-2 cancer cells showed that GQDs has higher toxicity than g-C3N4 nanosheets at the same concentration and treatment time. The IC50 for g-C3N4 nanosheets after 24 h treatment against Saos-2 cells was calculated as 66.8 ± 3.3 µg/mL. It seems that the oxygen functional groups on GQDs have a particular effect on the interactions of GQDs with cancer cells and cytotoxicity. The presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in the GQDs structure has caused more cellular uptake than g-C3N4 with amine functional groups.

The effect of cellular uptake of the bulk and GQDs in the morphology and viability of cancer cells was also studied using SEM microscopy (Fig. 4c). The treated cells show significant morphological changes with respect to the control experiment. These morphological changes in GQDs-treated cells are greater than that of the bulk-treated cells. However, both treated cells showed no apoptosis and necrosis cell destruction. These cell morphological changes might be partly due to osteogenesis or stem cell differentiation. GQDs penetrate across the cells membranes and accumulate in the cytoplasm, then they might interfere with certain cellular signaling pathways and induce the cancer cells differentiation. Differentiation might have led to cancer cells to lose their self-renewal property.

A possible mechanism in cell toxicity of GQDs might be explained by redox biology and the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Redox biology involves an increase in the ROS to its threshold level which activates signaling pathways and initiation of biological processes leading to differentiation of cancer cells [42,43]. High levels of ROS result in oxidative stress that might lead to cell damage. It appears that the GQDs enter the cells and induce ROS generation. When the ROS generation reaches a threshold level, the cell viability decreases. However, increasing ROS imparts pressure on cancer cells which leads to adaptation to strong antioxidant mechanisms [44]. Serious differentiation in cancer cells have resulted in normal tissue cells. The cell differentiation depends on physicochemical features of nanomaterials such as shape, size and surface charge [45]. Qin et al. [46] concluded that Ag nanoparticles with different sizes and concentrations have different effects on stem cell differentiation. Compared with the bulk, GQDs possess smaller sizes having a high potential for guiding cells toward differentiated cells. Winnik and Maysinger [47] demonstrated that quantum dots penetrate easily into the cell membrane and produce an oxidative stress by ROS generation. Zhou et al. [48] investigated the effect of oxygen-containing functional groups on the cytotoxicity of GQDs. Their results showed that the removal of some oxygen-containing functional groups on GQDs decreases cellular uptake. Yan et al. [49] found that the surface charge is a critical factor in cellular uptake of carbon quantum dots. Ruiz et al. [50] showed that GQD with high edge functional groups exhibits high antioxidant activity. Wang et al. [51] found that the scavenging efficiency of GQDs increases with the increase of oxygen groups. The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2c) reveals the presence of more oxygen-rich functional groups on GQDs than that of the bulk. The zeta potential measurements are also correlated with higher surface charge in the GQDs (Fig. 2b). The results suggest that size, concentration, and surface charge are important factors in biological properties of nanomaterials.

The high-speed electrophoretic size fractionation method was applied on a mega-bore capillary arrays for high-throughput preparation of GQDs. The electrophoretic fractionation of the bulk was completed in less than 10 s by applying a low electric field strength. The smallest collected fraction, GQDs, showed significant differences in their properties as compared to the bulk such as high PL intensity and bright luminescence. The results of cytotoxicity assessment showed the higher toxicity of GQDs as compared with the bulk against the bone cancer cells. The GQDs showed low toxicity at low concentrations (less than 50 μg/mL). At concentrations greater than 50 μg/mL, the cell viability decreased due to ROS generation. The results of cellular uptake showed that the GQDs induce more morphological changes as compared with the bulk probably due to significant osteogenic differentiation. The results of this work might be valuable for the use of GQDs in drug delivery, tissue engineering, bone reconstruction, and biomedical applications.

Maryam Davardoostmanesh: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Software, Writing - original draft. Hossein Ahmadzadeh: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Visualization. Elaheh K. Goharshadi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Azadeh Meshkini: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Elnaz Sistanipour: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge Ferdowsi University of Mashhad for supporting this project (grant number 3/46638).
